coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots using ionic liquid as a precursor and their application in Hg2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhuo, Kelei
Sun, Dong
Xu, Panpan
Wang, Chunfeng
Cao, Yingying
Chen, Yujuan
Liu, Jianming
coredata.dc:description: A facile and environment-friendly method was developed to synthesize sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots (S/N-CDs) via one step hydrothermal treatment of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium 2-amino-3-mercaptopropionic acid salt ionic liquid and polyethylene glycol. It was found that the prepared S/N-CDs were nearly spherical nanoparticles. And then the size of the as-prepared S/N-CDs became smaller with the extension of reaction time, the amorphous carbon was gradually transformed into a crystal structure of carbon dots and a higher reaction temperature favors the formation of carbon dots with higher quantum yields. It was also found that sulfur atoms in the S/N-CDs change the surface structures of CDs to some extent. Higher quantum yield of the S/N-CDs should attribute to the synergistic effect of co-doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms. The S/N-CDs display stable and strong florescence, high water solubility, excitation-dependent emission behavior, particularly the up-conversion photoluminescence performance. Furthermore, the as-prepared S/N-CDs were used as a sensitive probe for Hg2+ detection in aqueous solutions, with high selectivity and sensitivity. Particularly, the detection limit could reach as low as 0.6 nM (S/N=3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquid
Co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Hydrothermal treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the S/N-CDs for different reaction times: A, 3h; B, 9h; C, 12h. Insets are the magnification of a single particle.
XPS spectrum (A) of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C). High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C), O1s (D), and S2p (E) of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C).
FTIR spectrum (A) and XRD pattern (B) of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C).
UV–vis absorption spectra of the S/N-CDs synthesized under different conditions ((A) a: 3h, b: 9h, c: 12h; (B) a: 80°C, b: 140°C, c: 200°C).

(A) Optimal excitation and emission spectra of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) (inset is photograph taken under visible light). (B) fluorescence emission spectra of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) (with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 353 to 503nm in 10nm increment; inset is photograph taken under 365nm UV light).
Up-conversion photoluminescence spectra of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) at excitation wavelengths from 640 to 750nm in 10nm increment.
Effects of various conditions on the fluorescence intensity of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C): (A) pH; (B) storage time; (C) ionic strengths (ionic strengths controlled by various NaCl concentrations).

(A) The fluorescence intensity (F) of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) in the phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.01M, pH 7.0) after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ (c): 0, 0.002, 0.006, 0.025, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60μM. The insets show plots of the F/F
0 against c in the range of 2 to 100 nM and 10 to 60μM, respectively. Logarithmic plot of fluorescence quenching of S/N-CDs with c: (B) 2.0 to 100nM; (C) 10 to 60μM.

F/F
0 of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) in PBS (0.01M, pH 7.0) with 40µM different metal ions. F and F
0 correspond to the fluorescence intensities of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) with and without 40µM of different metal ions, respectively.
Fluorescence data and QY of the S/N-CDs synthesized under different conditions.
Performance comparison of the as-prepared S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) as a probe for the detection of Hg2+ with other carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots using ionic liquid as a precursor and their application in Hg2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University,

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science, Henan Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and environment-friendly method was developed to synthesize sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots (S/N-CDs) via one step hydrothermal treatment of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium 2-amino-3-mercaptopropionic acid salt ionic liquid and polyethylene glycol. It was found that the prepared S/N-CDs were nearly spherical nanoparticles. And then the size of the as-prepared S/N-CDs became smaller with the extension of reaction time, the amorphous carbon was gradually transformed into a crystal structure of carbon dots and a higher reaction temperature favors the formation of carbon dots with higher quantum yields. It was also found that sulfur atoms in the S/N-CDs change the surface structures of CDs to some extent. Higher quantum yield of the S/N-CDs should attribute to the synergistic effect of co-doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms. The S/N-CDs display stable and strong florescence, high water solubility, excitation-dependent emission behavior, particularly the up-conversion photoluminescence performance. Furthermore, the as-prepared S/N-CDs were used as a sensitive probe for Hg2+ detection in aqueous solutions, with high selectivity and sensitivity. Particularly, the detection limit could reach as low as 0.6 nM (S/N=3).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescent carbon materials with less than 10nm in size, have generated tremendous interest because of their unique properties and wide range of application in sensors [1–6], photocatalysts, bioimaging and optoelectronic devices [7–10]. However, the prepared CDs emits weak fluorescence at the beginning of the study, the quantum yield (QY) is less than 10%, which limits the application of CDs to some extent. Therefore, it is necessary to explore new synthetic approaches for preparing excellent optical performance CDs.

The heteroatom doping in CDs is an efficient method to enhance their photoluminescence properties by tuning their surface/local chemistry and electronic properties [11,12]. Yang et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped and highly fluorescent CDs using ammonium citrate as precursor, with a fluorescence QY about 13.5% [13]. Ding et al. synthesized heavy doped CDs with tunable-photoluminescence for sensitive fluorescence detection of metal ions and the highest QY of CDs is 39% [14]. Qian et al. synthesized Si-doped CDs by a simple strategy. The Si-doped CDs display strong photoluminescence with a QY of 19.2%, low cellular toxicity, and multifunctional sensing performance to determination of Fe(III), H2O2 and melamine. The reason may be due to fluorescence enhancement effect of introduced Si atoms into CDs [15]. These reports demonstrate that the heteroatom doping is a good approach to improve optical properties of CDs. Furthermore, the co-doped CDs can be prepared by doping different heteroatoms. The interaction between the dopant atoms can improve the optical performance of CDs. Dong et al. proposed a simple method for synthesizing sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped CDs with excitation-independent emission and high QY [16]. Hu et al. synthesized nitrogen/sulfur dopant CDs by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of the precursor rice as carbon source and N-acetyl-L-cysteine as N and S dopants [17].

Ionic liquids (ILs) are new materials. Some task-specified ILs have been used to build functional nanomaterials [18]. Recent reports showed that nitrogen-containing materials or molecules could be used for preparation of highly photoluminescent CDs [19–21]. Most ILs possesses organic nitrogen-containing bulky cation or inorganic heteroatom-containing anion and these may render them ideal molecular precursors for photoluminescent doped CDs. At present, using ILs as the precursor to synthesize heteroatom-co-doped CDs has rarely reported. Therefore, it is still a considerable challenge to establish facile methods for producing heteroatom-co-doped CDs by using ILs containing different heteroatoms as a precursor.

Herein, we propose a simple method to produce sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots (S/N-CDs) by one step hydrothermal treatment of 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium 2-amino-3-mercaptopropionic acid salt ([C4mim][Cys]) ionic liquid and polyethylene glycol (PEG2000N) for the first time. The as-prepared S/N-CDs showed high resistance to photobleaching, excellent water solubility, excitation-dependent emission behavior, as well as up-conversion photoluminescence performance. Furthermore, a higher reaction temperature favors the formation of carbon dots with higher quantum yields, and the doped sulfur atoms can improve the optical performance of CDs to some extent. The prepared nanomaterial was employed as a sensitive probe for Hg2+ detection with a high selectivity and sensitivity.

1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium 2-amino-3-mercaptopropionic acid (L-cysteine) salt ionic liquid, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium 2-aminopropionic acid (L-alanine) salt ([C4mim][Ala]) ionic liquid were prepared according to the procedure described in the literature [22]. In addition, all other reagents and apparatus used in this work are the same as those used in our previous work [23].

S/N-CDs were prepared via one step hydrothermal treatment of [C4mim][Cys] and PEG2000N. In detail, [C4mim][Cys] (0.3g) and PEG2000N (0.9g) were dissolved in 2.0ml of ultrapure water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 80°C, 140°C, or 200°C for 3h, 9h, or 12h, respectively. After cooling down to room temperature, the products were centrifuged at 18,000rpm for 30min. The optically transparent samples were dialyzed by molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 against ultrapure water for purification. Finally, the S/N-CDs samples were freeze-dried for characterization. For comparison, the N-CDs were synthesized with [C4mim][Ala] as a precursor at 200°C for 12h.

1mL of buffer solution (0.01M, pH 7.0), a calculated amount of Hg2+, and 10μL of the purified S/N-CDs solution were putted into a 2.0mL tube. Then the mixture was diluted to a given volume. The photoluminescence measurements were performed after 10min at room temperature.

The morphologies of three kinds of S/N-CDs (S/N-CDs (3h, 200°C), S/N-CDs (9h, 200°C) and S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C)) were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) ( Fig. 1). Fig. 1 shows that the three S/N-CDs are nearly spherical nanoparticles. While the S/N-CDs (9h, 200°C) and S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) have a small aggregation. S/N-CDs (3h, 200°C), S/N-CDs (9h, 200°C) and S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) have average diameters of 43.2, 18.5 and 12.6nm, respectively (100 random carbon dots were accounted for each sample). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TRTEM) images of the S/N-CDs (9h, 200°C) and S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) (insets of Fig. 1B and C) clearly reveal a good crystallinity with a lattice pacing of 0.208 and 0.214nm, respectively. These values are close to (100) diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [24]. These results indicate that two S/N-CDs possess a graphite-like structure. However, the HRTEM image of the S/N-CDs (3h, 200°C) do not reveal any clear lattice fringes, indicating its amorphous nature.

Fig. 2A is the X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C). There are four peaks at about 161.7, 285.0, 397.5 and 530.9eV, which are corresponding to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, and their contents are 0.38%, 68.13%, 1.17%, and 30.32%, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) is deconvoluted into three single peaks that attribute to C–C (283.5eV), C=C (284.8eV) and C–O/C–N/C–S (285.3eV) functional groups [17,25]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) reveals the existence of pyridinic N (397.5eV) and pyrrolic N (400.0eV) [21,26]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) confirms two characteristic oxygen states of C=O (530.6eV) and C–O (531.2eV) [26]. The S2p spectrum exhibits two peaks centered at 163.9 and 165.1eV (Fig. 1e), showing that sulfur exists in two forms [27].

The surface functional groups of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) were detected by fourier transform infrared (FTIR) ( Fig. 3A). The absorption band at 3360–3483cm−1 is assigned to the O–H and N–H [23], demonstrating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups on the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C). The peak at 2889cm−1 represents C–H stretching vibrations. The peak at 1632cm−1 is ascribed to the C=C and C=O bonds [28]. The peaks at 1573, 1469, and 1349cm−1 represent aromatic C–N and heterocyclic C–C compounds [29]. Furthermore, the broad band at 1062–1120cm−1 reveals the presence of the C–O–C, C–S and C–O bonds [28]. In addition, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) has a wide peak at ca. 22.3° (Fig. 3B), displaying that the interlayer spacing of (002) diffraction peak is 0.40nm. This value is slightly larger than that of graphite (0.34nm), which is ascribed to the introduction of nitrogen-, sulfur- and oxygen-containing groups [30].

Fig. 4A reveals the ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of the S/N-CDs synthesized under different reaction times. It was found that two absorption peaks at 250 and 300nm were observed for three kinds of S/N-CDs (S/N-CDs (3h, 200°C), S/N-CDs (9h, 200°C), and S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C)), meanwhile their intensities gradually increased. The peak at 250nm is assigned to π–π⁎ transition of the C=N bond [31], while the peak at 300 is ascribed to n–π⁎ transition of the C=O bond [13]. Fig. 4B reveals the UV–Vis absorption spectra of S/N-CDs synthesized at 80°C, 140°C and 200°C for 12h. With the reaction temperature increasing from 80 to 200°C, the absorption peak slightly blue-shifted from 260 to 250nm and their intensities gradually increased. Besides, the new absorption peak at 300nm appeared when the reaction temperature reached to 200°C (curve c). The results revealed that the reaction temperature and time can influence the absorption properties of the S/N-CDs.

Fig. 5 reveals the fluorescence spectra of S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C). In the fluorescence spectra, the strong emission wavelength is at 477nm (Fig. 5A). The insets of Fig. 5A and B show the optical images of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C). The dispersion is colorless under visible light. However, it emits bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation. Similar to the CDs previously reported [28,32], the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) also exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior. When the excitation wavelengths vary from 353 to 503nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths for the S/N-CDs (Fig. 5B) shift from 433 to 536nm. At the same time, the intensities of emission peaks first increase to a maximum (at 477nm) and then decrease.

Interestingly, the up-conversion photoluminescence behaviors of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) are different from their corresponding down-conversion excitation-dependent photoluminescence behaviors. Fig. 6 shows that the up-conversion photoluminescence spectra of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) have a fixed emission peak at 443nm and their intensities increased with increasing the excitation wavelengths from 640 to 750nm. The fixed emission location exhibited that the emission occurs from the lowest single state irrespective of the mode of excitation [33]. The up-conversion photoluminescence properties of the CDs can be due to the multi-photon activation process [34]. It has a great advantage in biological imaging applications.

Table 1 gives the fluorescence data and QYs of the S/N-CDs synthesized under different conditions. The highest QY of the S/N-CDs is 13.71%. We note that the increase of reaction temperature from 80 to 200°C leads to an increase in the QY of the synthesized S/N-CDs from 1.99 to 13.71%, which is consistent with the reported results in the literature [21]. The extension of the reaction time favors the formation of the CDs with a high QY. It was found that the reaction temperature and time can also influence the photoluminescence properties of the S/N-CDs. The difference in QYs and optical properties may be caused by the different carbonization degree. Furthermore, it was found that the water content also affects the QY of the CDs. Using a suitable water content, we can obtain the CDs with a high QY (Table S1).

Next, we further investigate the effect of different conditions on fluorescent properties of the CDs: (pH, storage time and ionic strengths). Firstly, Fig. 7A displays that the photoluminescence intensity has a little change with increasing pH value from 1.0 to 12.0, and then decreases sharply when pH>12, meaning that the fluorescence quenching occurs at pH>12. A possible reason is that carboxylic groups on the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) lose protons to form a large number of carboxyl anions at pH>12. Then the surrounded S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) by anion aggregate, which lead to fluorescence quench [13]. Secondly, the photoluminescence intensities only have slightly decrease under UV irradiation exposure for 180min, indicating that the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) have highly resistant to photobleaching (Fig. 7B). Thirdly, their photoluminescence intensities almost remain unvarying when NaCl concentrations gradually increase, exhibiting a good stability of the S/N-CDs in high ionic strength (Fig. 7C).

For comparison, we also synthesized the N-CDs. For the N-CDs, only an absorption peak at 260nm is observed in the ultraviolet-visible spectrum, while the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) have two absorption peaks at 250 and 300nm (Fig S1a). The N-CDs have a strong fluorescence emission peak at 416nm (Fig S1b), while the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) have the highest emission wavelength at 477nm. The QY of the N-CD is 7.21%, while that of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) reaches to 13.71%. We could hypothesize that the introduction of sulfur leads to a different surface structure of CDs and thus improve the QY of the CDs.

Next, we discuss the effect of the surface structure of CDs on their optical performance. XPS results display that the N-CDs are composed of C, N and O (Fig. S2). The element contents in the N-CDs and the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) are given in Table S2. It was found that the introduction of sulfur leads to the promotion of QY. FTIR spectra of the N-CDs further indicate the existence of C–O, C–O–C, C=O, C–N, O–H, and C–C groups, without C–S (Fig. S3a). In addition, there is a wide peak at 23.5° in the XRD pattern of the N-CDs (Fig. S3b). Thus it can be seen that the interlayer spacing of (002) diffraction peak in the N-CDs is less than that in the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C), which may be due to the fact that the introduction of sulfur into the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) leads to a larger lattice spacing. From above discussions, we can conclude that the surface structure and composition of CDs determines their optical properties. The doped sulfur changes the composition and surface structure of CDs. The high quantum yield of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) is due to the synergistic effect of co-doped N and S [31].

Fig. 8A showed that the S/N-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescence emission peak at 477nm in the absence of Hg2+ ions. However, with the addition of different Hg2+ amounts, the fluorescence intensity of the S/N-CDs decreases in some degree, showing that the sensing system is sensitive to different Hg2+ concentrations. Insets in Fig. 8A show the dependence of F/F
0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions, where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. In given concentration range, the quenching of the fluorescence intensity by Hg2+could also be described using equation [35]:
(1)
log
[ (
F
0
− F ) / F ] ) =
log
K + n
log
c
where K and n are the binding constant and the number of binding sites, respectively, c is Hg2+ concentration. Two good linear correlations were respectively obtained over two concentration ranges from 2.0 to 100nM: log [(F 0-F)/F]=0.665log c–0.726 (R2=0.9873) (Fig. 8B) and from 10 to 60μM: log [(F 0-F)/F]=1.39log c–2.63 (R2=0.9933) (Fig. 8C). Thus, the K can be obtained from the intercept: K=1.88×105 L/mol (2.0–100nM), K=2.5×103 L/mol (10–60μM). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.6 nM (S/N=3). The analytical performances of the method proposed by us and other authors [28,36–46] are summarized in Table 2.

Different metal ions were investigated under the optimal conditions. Fig. 9 showed that no obvious photoluminescence quenching was observed for the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) after the addition of Ca2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, or Ag+. The excellent performance of the as-prepared CDs may be due to the strong interactions between Hg2+ and the functional groups on the S/N-CDs, leading to a fluorescence quenching through electronic or energy transfer [47].

Next, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) solutions in the absence and presence of Hg2+ were determined. In Fig. S4, the intensity of the absorption peak at 250nm of the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) solutions also decreased obviously upon the addition of Hg2+. Therefore, we found that Hg2+ has a stronger thermodynamic affinity with “N” groups existing on the S/N-CDs (12h, 200°C) than other metal ions.

A simple approach was developed for the first time to prepare sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots by one step hydrothermal treatment of [C4mim][Cys] and PEG2000N. The S/N-CDs display excellent solubility, stable and strong photoluminescence, excitation-dependent emission behavior as well as the up-conversion photoluminescence performance. The sizes of S/N-CDs became smaller with the extension of reaction time, and a higher reaction temperature favors the formation of carbon dots with higher quantum yields. The doped sulfur atoms can improve the optical performance of CDs to some extent. In addition, the S/N-CDs can employ as a fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+ in aqueous solution with a high selectivity and sensitivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21573058, 21173070, U1504212, 21303044, 21573057) and Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Henan Province (15IRTSTHN 003) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.03.022.

ce:section.ce:para:

.

Synthesis of gold nanoparticles modified with ionic liquid based on the imidazolium cation
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups

Room temperature ionic liquids from 20 natural amino acids
Simple and green synthesis of nitrogen-, sulfur-, and phosphorus-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties and sensing application
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Carbon dot cluster as an efficient “off–on” fluorescent probe to detect Au (III) and glutathione
A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots

Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
One-step synthesis of high quantum-yield and excitation-independent emission carbon dots for cell imaging
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Facile preparation and upconversion luminescence of graphene quantum dots
Interactions of human serum albumin with chlorogenic acid and ferulic acid

Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a "turn-off' fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions
Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg (II) ions detection and cell imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of highly luminescent co-doped carbon nanodots for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+
A hydrothermal route for synthesizing highly luminescent sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots as nanosensors for Hg2+
